title: Cat Urinating in Sinks or Bathtubs: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat peeing in the sink or tub? Learn if it's an emergency, common causes like UTIs or stress, and how to help your cat today.
content_html:
TL;DR: Cats urinating in sinks or tubs (periuria) usually have underlying medical issues like UTIs or bladder stones and should see a vet within 24–48 hours; however, if they cannot produce urine, it is a life-threatening emergency.

Why is my cat urinating in the sink or bathtub?

  • Periuria: This is the medical term for a cat urinating in unusual, smooth-surfaced locations.
  • Communication: This behavior is often a signal that your cat is experiencing discomfort or has developed an aversion to the litter box due to pain.
  • Common Medical Causes: Potential issues include Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs), Feline Idiopathic Cystitis (FIC), bladder stones, or environmental stress.

Is it an emergency if my cat urinates in the bathtub or sink?

  • Low Urgency: If your cat is successfully passing urine, it is generally not an immediate threat, but they should be seen by a vet within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Critical Emergency: If your cat is straining, crying out, or unable to produce any urine, seek emergency veterinary care immediately to treat a potential life-threatening urethral obstruction.

How does a photo of the urine help my veterinarian?

  • Enhanced Visibility: Because sinks and tubs are typically white, a photo can clearly reveal if the urine contains blood (pink or red tint) or sediment like sand (crystals).
  • Faster Triage: Providing visual evidence helps your veterinarian assess the severity of inflammation and plan diagnostic steps before the physical exam begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When cats urinate in sinks or bathtubs, evaluating the behavioral history is the primary process to determine the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment plan. Important historical aspects include whether the elimination is urine or feces, if urine deposits are vertical (spraying) or horizontal (soiling), the problem's duration and frequency, signalment and temperament of the cat, when and where the cat eliminates, litter box details (number, placement, cleaning, substrate, size), the cat's daily routine, and home environment. It's important to consider conditions associated with feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD), such as urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, urolithiasis, and sterile cystitis. Diagnostic tests such as urinalysis, urine culture, radiography, and ultrasonography may be required to differentiate uroliths from urinary tract infection or neoplasia.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1531)

faqs.question: Is my cat doing this to be spiteful?
faqs.answer: No, cats do not urinate outside the box out of spite. They are usually trying to communicate physical pain or significant environmental stress.

faqs.question: Can stress cause a cat to pee in the sink?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress can lead to Feline Idiopathic Cystitis (FIC), which causes bladder inflammation and leads to urinating in strange places.

faqs.question: Should I change the litter box immediately?
faqs.answer: While a clean box is important, you should consult a vet first to rule out a medical issue. If the cat associates the box with pain, they will continue to avoid it until the medical cause is treated.
